name: Certificate Expiry Response
description: Identify and remediate expiring or expired certificates on domain controllers
author: AD Identity Control Plane

steps.title: Check DC Certificate Status
steps.description: List all certificates on the domain controller and check expiry dates.
steps.script: Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Select-Object Subject,NotAfter,Issuer | Sort-Object NotAfter

steps.title: Certificate Status
steps.description: What is the state of the DC certificates?

steps.title: Schedule Certificate Renewal
steps.description: Certificate is expiring soon. If auto-enrollment is configured, it should renew automatically. Check: 1) Certificate auto-enrollment GPO is applied, 2) The CA is online and has a valid signing certificate, 3) The certificate template allows auto-enrollment. If auto-enrollment is not configured, plan a manual renewal during maintenance window.

steps.title: Certificates Are Valid
steps.description: All DC certificates are within their validity period. Review the earliest expiry date and set a reminder for 30 days before. Ensure certificate auto-enrollment is configured to prevent future issues.

steps.title: Expired DC / LDAPS Certificate
steps.description: An expired DC certificate will break LDAPS connections and may affect smart card authentication. Immediate actions: 1) Check if the Enterprise CA is healthy, 2) Request a new DC certificate from the CA, 3) If CA is unavailable, consider a self-signed certificate as temporary measure. Note: LDAP (port 389) still works without a valid certificate.

steps.title: Expired Kerberos Certificate
steps.description: An expired Kerberos certificate affects PKINIT and smart card logon. Standard password-based Kerberos is unaffected. To remediate: 1) Ensure the KDC certificate template is published, 2) Force certificate auto-enrollment, 3) Verify the new certificate has the KDC EKU (1.3.6.1.5.2.3.5).

steps.title: Expired CA Certificate - Critical
steps.description: An expired CA certificate is a high-impact event. All certificates issued by this CA will be untrusted. Actions: 1) DO NOT delete the old CA certificate, 2) Renew the CA certificate (same key or new key depending on policy), 3) Publish the renewed certificate to AD, 4) Wait for replication, 5) Force Group Policy update on DCs. This may require a change control process.

steps.title: Verify Auto-Enrollment
steps.description: Check that certificate auto-enrollment is properly configured: 1) Open GPMC and check for auto-enrollment policy, 2) The policy should be linked to the Domain Controllers OU, 3) Certificate templates must have 'Autoenroll' permission for Domain Controllers. Force enrollment with: certutil -pulse

steps.title: Verify New Certificate
steps.description: Confirm the new certificate is installed and valid.

steps.title: Document and Set Reminders
steps.description: Record: 1) Which certificates were affected, 2) Root cause (auto-enrollment failure, CA issue, template misconfiguration), 3) Remediation steps, 4) Set calendar reminders for 30 days before next expiry, 5) Consider implementing certificate monitoring.
